title: Growth Curve Stress Test
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"c8sna","text":"This is one (fairly quantitative and reproducible) way to run a yeast growth study to analyze the effect of a stressor on yeast. It is not the only way! It is also less of a \"protocol\" and more of a \"template\" -- it measures the growth of a single yeast strain at one concentration of \"chemical X.\" Makes sure to adapt it to your own needs!","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"bch0k","text":"Remember, if you see a difference between the knockout and the wild-type under stress, it could be that the stress is slowing down the knockout strain. However, there's another possible explanation: it could just be that the knockout strain is growing more slowly under all conditions. Hence the no-stress controls.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"6erub","text":"Yeast and yeast media are not hazardous. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bt6g6","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4dg24","text":"However, you may be using a hazardous \"chemical X.\" Make sure to use appropriate PPE and engineering controls and dispose of it appropriately.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4g9at","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"23eu","text":"The yeast we are using are genetically modified. Make sure to inactivate them (by bleach or autoclaving, for example) before disposing of them.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"3d41","text":"Equipment","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6f7lo","text":"Vortexer","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"chl7s","text":"Microplate reader (with incubation and shaking)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8sb03","text":"Sterile workspace (such as a biosafety cabinet or laminar flow hood)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3u3iq","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5cqr0","text":"Materials","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":9}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2cbs6","text":"Corning COSTAR 96-well clear flat-bottom assay plate","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"a252k","text":"Wild-type yeast in double-strength synthetic complete media, OD600 ~ 0.1-0.2","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fahho","text":"Knockout yeast in double-strength synthetic complete media, OD600 ~ 0.1-0.2","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5a562","text":"A solution of Chemical X in water","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: If possible, perform steps 1 and 2 in a sterile environment such as a biosafety cabinet or laminar flow hood. Vortex the yeast culture briefly to resuspend the yeast cells.
Set up four wells according to the following table: %s %s Well 1Well 2Yeast culture50 µl50 µlChemical X, 100 mM10 µl--Water40 µl50 µl
Set up the plate reader as follows: Temperature: 30°C Mode: Kinetic Wavelength: 600 nm Interval: 5 minutes Total run time: 24 hours Shake before read: 30 seconds
Transfer the assay plate to the reader and read for 24 hours.
Plot each growth curve with the Y axis scale set to "logarithmic."
Find the part of the curve that is linear. In this case, I might choose from 250 minutes to 350 minutes.
Find the OD600 reading at these two times. At 250 minutes, my OD600 is 0.2086; at 350 minutes, my OD600 is 0.3583.

Compute the doubling time using a bit of math: Where t 1 and t 2 are the start and end times you found in step 2, and q 1 and q 2 are the OD600 at those times. Thus, for my BY4735 strain growing in YPD, the doubling time is 128 minutes.
creator: Brian Teague
